Shawnee State University · For Dean Miller & President · Confidential
Mid-GDC Field Update
Game Developers Conference · San Francisco · March 2026 · Prepared by Garrett Davis

ShawneeXP teams are mid-conference at GDC 2026 — and the results are already exceeding expectations. Here is a real-time update on what's happened in the first half of the week.

Publisher Meetings · GDC

5 Confirmed Next Steps with Publishers

Our graduate studios have been moving through back-to-back publisher meetings all week. As of mid-conference, 5 publishers have confirmed next steps — meaning they are sending our studios' games to their internal evaluation teams following GDC to formally pursue publishing agreements. This is the critical conversion point in the publisher pipeline: it moves a studio from "interesting" to "in review."

Additional meetings with publishers are scheduled for the remaining days of the conference, with more next steps expected before the week closes.

5Publisher Next Steps
4+Studios Active
2Days Remaining
Investor Relations · Major Development

Xsolla Chairman Verbally Agrees to Invest in ShawneeXP Fund

Shurick Agapitov, Founder & Chairman of Xsolla, has verbally agreed to invest in the ShawneeXP venture fund currently in development. Tom Sperry — who is being considered to head the fund — facilitated the introduction. Tom and Shurick were college roommates. Tom has requested a follow-up conversation with Eric to discuss the details.

Who is Xsolla?

Xsolla is the world's leading video game commerce and payments platform — the infrastructure that powers how games are sold, monetized, and distributed globally. Its clients include Epic Games (Fortnite), Ubisoft, Twitch, Roblox, and thousands of indie and AAA studios worldwide. Founded in 2005, Xsolla processes billions of dollars in game transactions annually and operates offices in Los Angeles, Berlin, Seoul, and Dubai.

The company has been valued at up to $3 billion and is the dominant force in game payments infrastructure globally — a position it holds across mobile, PC, and console ecosystems.

Who is Shurick Agapitov?

Aleksandr "Shurick" Agapitov is the Founder and Chairman of Xsolla and the AGP Family of companies spanning gaming, fintech, publishing, and Web3. Born in Perm, Russia in 1984, he began his entrepreneurial career at age 15 building an online currency exchange. He went on to build Xsolla into a multibillion-dollar global business from the ground up.

Shurick holds a degree in Mathematics and Computer Science from Perm State University and has completed executive education at Harvard, Wharton, and UCLA. He is also the co-founder of 80 Level, one of the most widely read game development media platforms in the world, and author of Once Upon Tomorrow, a book on the Metaverse and the future of creativity. He describes himself publicly as a billionaire and has been featured in Bloomberg, Entrepreneur, and Boss Magazine.

His investment in the ShawneeXP fund would represent a landmark validation of the program by one of the most connected and resourced figures in the global games industry.

Competition Win · Pocket Gamer Connects SF

JollyWare Wins the Very Big Indie Pitch 🏆

🦇

Gnomad — 1st Place, Very Big Indie Pitch

Elijah Wickerham, Co-Founder of JollyWare, won first place at the Very Big Indie Pitch at Pocket Gamer Connects San Francisco — one of the most prestigious indie game pitch competitions in the industry. The prize: the coveted golden baseball bat, awarded only to winners of the Big Indie Pitch, editorial coverage across PocketGamer.biz, PCGamesInsider, and The Virtual Report, promotional packages, and free passes to a future PG Connects event.

What is the Very Big Indie Pitch? The Very Big Indie Pitch is PocketGamer's flagship speed-pitch competition, held at their major global conference events. Indie developers pitch their games in a speed-dating-style format to a rotating panel of industry experts — publishers, investors, and press. Only the best games in the room advance, and only one walks away with the bat. It is one of the most competitive indie pitching arenas in the world, with developers attending from dozens of countries. The golden bat is one of the most recognized symbols of indie game achievement in the industry — acquirable only by winning.

Elijah Wickerham · JollyWare · Very Big Indie Pitch Winner · PG Connects San Francisco 2026
Exclusive Event · GDC Week

The GDP — GDC's Most Exclusive Private Gathering

ShawneeXP teams are attending The GDP — an invitation-only private dinner and networking event held annually during GDC week. This is not a conference event. It is a curated, guest-list-only gathering hosted at a private residence in San Francisco's SOMA district — a converted 1923 warehouse home belonging to Chris and Carrie Jurney — and co-hosted by veteran industry connector Rich Bisso.

Access is strict: No walk-ins, no last-minute additions, valid ID required, 21+ only. Attendance is by personal invitation from the hosts — it is one of the most difficult invitations to obtain in the games industry.

The GDP brings together approximately 300 of the most senior figures across game development, publishing, investment, and technology — a room that includes CEOs of major studios, partners at top VC firms, engineering fellows at Epic Games and Meta, founders of iconic franchises, and government trade representatives. ShawneeXP and our studios are in that room — including Elijah Wickerham (CEO, JollyWare) and Devan Mallory (CEO, Cyquential Studios), attending as founders of ShawneeXP graduate companies. Elijah arrives as the freshly crowned Very Big Indie Pitch winner — making their presence in that room even more notable.

Notable attendees from the 2025 guest list include figures from Epic Games, Meta, Insomniac Games, Schell Games, Q-Games, Crate Entertainment, Iron Galaxy, Spry Fox, Oculus Publishing, Obsidian Entertainment, and many more — alongside investors, attorneys, composers, and the General Consul of Mexico. ShawneeXP's own Marcus Cenci and Charley Harbord also appear on the guest list, reflecting the depth of our industry network.

Program Momentum

What This Week Means

In the first half of GDC alone, ShawneeXP has: secured 5 publisher next steps for graduate studios, obtained a verbal investment commitment from the chairman of a $3B company for our emerging venture fund, won one of the most competitive indie pitch competitions in the world, and placed our teams inside some of the most exclusive professional gatherings the games industry holds. Two days remain.

This is Portsmouth, Ohio on the world stage. We will have a full post-conference summary once the week concludes.

For Review · Dean Miller & President

What We Need to Capitalize on This Momentum

The work happening at GDC this week is creating real, time-sensitive opportunities. Below are the specific resources and decisions we are bringing to leadership to discuss. Acting quickly on these items will determine whether we can fully convert this week's momentum into lasting outcomes for the program and the university.

01
Contact Tom Sperry — Venture Fund Development

Xsolla Chairman Shurick Agapitov has verbally agreed to invest in the ShawneeXP venture fund. Tom Sperry — who is being considered to head the fund — facilitated this relationship and has specifically requested a conversation with Eric to work through the structure and details of the fund and Tom's involvement.

This is a time-sensitive window. Verbal commitments at GDC move fast and need to be followed up while energy is high. We are asking that Eric connect with Tom Sperry directly as soon as possible to begin those conversations.

Action needed: Eric contacts Tom Sperry to discuss fund structure and his role. Garrett can facilitate the introduction and provide context.

02
Support for Studio Evaluation Packages — Next Week

With 5 publishers now sending our studios to their evaluation teams post-GDC, the work begins immediately next week. Publishers' evaluation processes are fast-moving and unforgiving — studios that arrive unprepared with their materials lose the opportunity entirely.

Our teams will need intensive support to finalize their budget decks, demo packages, and supporting materials to the standard that evaluation teams expect. We are requesting authorization and funding to bring in the following ShawneeXP mentors and partners on a paid basis to support the teams through this critical window:

Marcus Cenci
Trouble Makers — Branding, visual identity, and pitch deck design. Marcus knows our studios and their materials better than anyone outside the program.
Charley Harbord
Powell Group — Publisher relations and evaluation strategy. Charley sits on the PG Connects board and has direct relationships with the publishers now evaluating our teams.
Chris Volpe
ShawneeXP Mentor — Business development and budget strategy. Chris is already on the ground at GDC with our teams and understands exactly what each studio needs.

Action needed: Authorization and budget to engage Marcus Cenci, Charley Harbord, and Chris Volpe for post-GDC studio support. Scope and rates to be confirmed — we are requesting approval in principle to move quickly.

03
ShawneeXP Rebrand — A Decision That Needs Leadership Direction

This is perhaps the most important conversation we need to have. We are not sending out any of the communications or materials developed this week until we have leadership's blessing on the brand.

The current ShawneeXP visual identity — logos, color palette, and design language — was created by Marcus Cenci and Trouble Makers as placeholder assets while the program found its footing. They were never intended to be permanent. We understand there are concerns at the leadership level about this branding, and we take that seriously.

The challenge is this: a quick, reactive rebrand creates its own risks. Our industry partners, studios, mentors, and conference contacts have all been introduced to ShawneeXP under the current identity. An abrupt visual change without intention and quality behind it signals instability — and in a relationship-driven industry, that matters. The rebrand needs to be done once, properly, and to a standard that reflects both the university and the program's national ambitions.

What we are asking:

▶  What does leadership want ShawneeXP to look and feel like? Direction on tone, visual standards, and alignment with university brand guidelines.

▶  Who should lead the rebrand? Should it be handled internally, by Marcus Cenci with new direction, or by another design partner?

▶  What is the timeline and budget? We want to do this right — not fast.

▶  Until the rebrand is complete, we are holding all external-facing materials and communications pending leadership approval on a case-by-case basis.
